coredata.dc:title: Engineering mitoxantrone-conjugated graphene quantum dots for advanced photoluminescent cancer therapy

coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the optoelectronic and photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) conjugated with the Mitoxantrone (MTX) drug, aiming to enhance cancer drug delivery systems. Using DFT and TDDFT numerical simulations, we examined the impact of GQD size and MTX coupling positions (C–C, C–O, and C–N bonds) on the physical behaviour of DSGQDs. Our study confirm that all structures are energetically stable, as evidenced by the absence of negative frequencies in the IR spectra. Besides, C–O conjugated structures are found to be the most chemically stable as it was shown by global reactivity indices results. Functionalization leads to a reduced H–L energy gap, depending on the MTX coupling position and GQD size. Importantly, the optical properties of GQDs remain largely unaffected by conjugation, maintaining their stability as drug carriers. Larger GQD systems retained stable absorption and photoluminescence characteristics within the near-infrared (NIR) range, while smaller systems exhibited a shift from the visible to the NIR range. Notably, the C48H18 + MTX system with C–O bonding demonstrated superior photoluminescence performance confirmed by the small line width results. These results suggest that GQDs conjugated with MTX hold significant potential for targeted cancer therapy, with minimal interaction between the drug and the GQD host, thereby reducing toxicity risks.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the optoelectronic and photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) conjugated with the Mitoxantrone (MTX) drug, aiming to enhance cancer drug delivery systems. Using DFT and TDDFT numerical simulations, we examined the impact of GQD size and MTX coupling positions (C–C, C–O, and C–N bonds) on the physical behaviour of DSGQDs. Our study confirm that all structures are energetically stable, as evidenced by the absence of negative frequencies in the IR spectra. Besides, C–O conjugated structures are found to be the most chemically stable as it was shown by global reactivity indices results. Functionalization leads to a reduced H–L energy gap, depending on the MTX coupling position and GQD size. Importantly, the optical properties of GQDs remain largely unaffected by conjugation, maintaining their stability as drug carriers. Larger GQD systems retained stable absorption and photoluminescence characteristics within the near-infrared (NIR) range, while smaller systems exhibited a shift from the visible to the NIR range. Notably, the C48H18 + MTX system with C–O bonding demonstrated superior photoluminescence performance confirmed by the small line width results. These results suggest that GQDs conjugated with MTX hold significant potential for targeted cancer therapy, with minimal interaction between the drug and the GQD host, thereby reducing toxicity risks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of quantum dots (QDs), particularly those originating from 2D honeycomb lattices [1–3], has generated intense interest in a broad range of scientific research and applications. These tiny fragments come in various shapes, such as hexagonal [4] and rectangular [5] form, to suit and align with the required applications. Known for their exceptional physical, chemical, and biological qualities, QDs possess a distinctive array of characteristics, including biocompatibility, small size, impressive photostability, easy functionalization, highly tunable photoluminescence, electrochemiluminescence, and chemical inertness [6–8]. Additionally, they can disperse better in solvents [9].

Graphene-based QDs differ from their 2D analogues primarily in terms of dimensionality and size [10,11]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) still possess a honeycomb structure inside the dots which explains their remarkable properties inherited especially from 2D graphene sheet [12]. GQDs, synthesized for the first time in 2008 [13], exhibit a semiconductor behaviour due to their larger band gap [14], which results from quantum confinement effects induced by their nanoscale dimensions and zero-dimensional structure [13]. Interestingly, the band gap is a key feature that has brought significant attention to GQDs, as it can be precisely controlled by adjusting their size [15,16]. This makes their band gap tunable by increasing or decreasing their size. The HOMO–LUMO (H–L) gap energy decreased with increasing the size in diamond-shaped graphene quantum dots [17,18]. Comparable electronic characteristics were identified in a series of GQDs samples with various diameter size from 5 to 35 nm, which were experimentally fabricated through the hydrothermal cutting of graphene sheets [19]. Additionally, with this change of band gap, the QDs can possess excellent photoluminescence properties and can emit light in a wide range of wavelengths, including ultraviolet (UV) [20] and infrared (IR) [21].

Adjusting edge and surface functionalization of GQDs’ structure is also a crucial tool to manipulate their properties for specific applications [22], employing various kinds of atoms or chemical groups [23,24]. Pristine GQDs and silicene QDs, when functionalized with chemical groups such as OH, CH3, and COOH, exhibit a remarkable and intriguing variety of electronic and optical properties [25]. Similarly, the theoretical results on biocompatibility, internalization efficiency, toxicity profiles, and fluorescence emission properties obtained from doping GQDs with various heteroatoms including nitrogen [26,27], boron [28], phosphorus [29], sulfur [30], and others [31,32], strongly support their synthesis for applications in bioimaging within the visible and NIR spectrum. Furthermore, these doped GQDs show great promise for in vitro cancer detection based on pH levels [30].

Notably, the unique features of QDs, including the nanoscale size and the ability to modify their surfaces with specific functional groups, have advanced their use as effective carriers for therapeutic agents [33–35], sensing [36–38], and cancer cell tracking [39,40]. Particularly, drug delivery involves transporting therapeutic substances to specific target cancer sites within the body [41,42]. QDs nanomaterials were proven to be safe for living organisms, had the right structure and can strongly absorb other substances [43]. Precisely, GQDs were successfully synthesized from graphene-oxide via refluxing with concentrated nitric acid and subsequently functionalized with arginine–glycine–aspartic acid (RGD) [44]. From the other hand, drug doxorubicin (DOX) which is an anticancer drug, was loaded onto the GQDs-RGD surfaces, with a loading efficiency of about 54.6%, and revealed efficient uptake of DOX-GQDs-RGD by glioma cells. In another experimental work, strongly green fluorescent GQDs-PEG were synthesized with the intention of serving as carriers for drug delivery [45]. These nanomaterials demonstrated a high PL quantum yield, reaching 18.8%, which is crucial for effective tracking and targeted delivery of therapeutic agents in biological systems. Theoretical approaches, including density functional theory (DFT) calculations, showed that the binding energy between the drug and centre nitrogen-doped-GQDs was greater than that of pristine GQDs and edge N-GQDs, indicating enhanced drug adsorption on the nanocarriers, and notable increase in optical behaviour and photoluminescence [46].

Additionally, GQDs can also be used as smart biosensors for cancer diagnosis [47]. Notably, cancer diagnostic biosensors are engineered to identify and transform specific cancer-related substances, including antibodies and proteins present on the surface of cancer cells into detectable signals [48]. NSE/anti-NSE/amine-N-GQDs @ AuNPs fluorescent turn-on biosensor were synthesized for highly sensitive detection of the small cell lung cancer biomarker [49]. The biosensor had a rapid response time (16 min), a wide linear detection range (0.1 pg
mL
− 1
to 1000 ng
mL
− 1
), low detection limit (0.09 pg
mL
− 1
), and high selectivity.

Driven by the groundbreaking potential of GQDs in biomedical applications, we aim to explore the transformative effects of functionalizing GQDs with an organic molecule. Our main objective is to identify the most effective system with optimal electronic and optical properties for enhancing drug delivery processes. Understanding how functionalization impacts the carrier-host dynamics, can lead to more efficient and safer therapeutic applications. To improve the accuracy of our study, we used two different sizes of GQDs and tested various methods for linking them to the drug, taking advantage of differences in atomic composition at the edges. We then optimized each system to carefully analyse their electronic and optical behaviours, with a particular emphasis on photoluminescence properties.

The employed DSGQD are:
C
30
H
14
and
C
48
H
18
. This choice is justified based on the observation that larger GQDs, such as GQD92 and GQD122, exhibited similar properties to those of 2D graphene sheet and that quantum confinement effect is very prominent for smallest structure [17]. After functionalization, the same behaviour observed for larger GQDs was also noted in
C
48
H
18
. For this matter, we carefully selected a smaller and larger size. Furthermore, several studies focused on exactly these two sizes [17,50,51], but none explored their potential in drug delivery applications. Additionally, biocompatibility is crucial in drug delivery, and a study has demonstrated that smaller GQDs, such as GQD-PEG, show excellent in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility due to their small size and high oxygen content. Meanwhile, larger graphene-based nanomaterials, such as GO-PEG, can exhibit toxicity due to aggregation and thrombus formation [52]. Therefore, selecting smaller GQDs ensures a safer profile for potential biomedical applications. Thus, we aim to continue along this path, assessing their potential in medical application, specifically drug delivery. Furthermore, by focusing on these two sizes, we aim to highlight the most relevant size-dependent effects while maintaining a clear and focused analysis. This approach ensures that the impact of GQD size is thoroughly investigated within the context of our study. Our findings reveal that the energetic stability of the DSGQDs+MTX systems and the reduction in the H–L energy gap are significantly influenced by both the size of the GQDs and the functionalization patterns. Notably, larger GQDs maintain stable absorption and photoluminescence in the near-infrared (NIR) range, while smaller GQDs shift from the visible to NIR ranges. Importantly, the intrinsic properties of the GQDs remain largely intact post-conjugation, indicating a minimal toxicity risk. These results highlight the potential of GQDs+MTX conjugates as innovative candidates for targeted cancer therapy, capable of maintaining drug efficacy while reducing the adverse side effects typically associated with conventional drug delivery methods.

The paper is organized as follows: The first section details the computational methodologies employed. The second section presents the results obtained, accompanied by a thorough discussion of the principal findings. Finally, the concluding section synthesizes the key insights, encapsulating the core essence of our investigation.

Our computational analyses were carried out using the Gaussian 09 software package. The molecular structures of the Mitoxantrone-conjugated GQDs were optimized using density functional theory (DFT) in conjunction with the broadly-applied B3LYP exchange–correlation method, complemented by the 6-31G(d,p) basis set [53–56]. The B3LYP method is widely preferred for its accuracy in computing electronic properties of large molecules [57], and for its accurate functionals in computational chemistry [58,59]. Furthermore, the B3LYP method, known for its balance of computational efficiency and accuracy, has previously been employed to investigate large molecular systems. It has been shown that the B3LYP method was employed and found to be the most accurate for studying GQDs containing up to 170 atoms, compared to other methods like pure gradient approximation (GGA) functional (PBE), hybrid-GGA functionals (PBE0), hybrid metaGGA (TPSSh), and long-range-corrected functionals (CAMB3LYP and LC-wPBE) [60]. Similar to the aforementioned work, 6-31G(d,p) basis set was selected for the present work, since we investigate smaller sized GQDs [60]. This method has also shown great promise in the field of drug delivery, studying a combined large system of borospherene and metalloborospherenes [61].

The B3LYP methodology combines multiple theoretical frameworks, integrating elements from Hartree–Fock (HF) exchange with both local and non-local correlation functions, resulting in a more precise estimation of exchange–correlation energy. Additionally, the method employs the Vosko, Wilk, and Nusair (VWN) functional III [62] for local correlation, alongside the Lee, Yang, and Parr (LYP) correlation functional for non-local correlation. The formulation of the exchange–correlation energy within the B3LYP functional, as delineated by Becke [63], involves a systematic summation of constituent energies. Thus, Eq. (2.1) is presented as follows:

As a first step in the calculation process, we optimized all the structures in the ground state. Additionally, the energy and frequency in the ground state were calculated to determine the HOMO and LUMO values, as well as the vibrational frequencies. For UV–Vis analysis, we performed calculations using TD-SCF to examine excited states, selecting between 15 and 20 states based on the size of the system under study.

We specifically selected the singlet state for spin configurations during these calculations. The ground state in graphene based systems is often a singlet because it corresponds to the lowest energy state, where all electrons are paired. Since the ground state of most graphene-based systems is non-magnetic, the singlet state is preferred because minimizes the energy due to electron pairing, making it more stable configuration [64,65].

To evaluate the stability of our system, namely, the GQDs attached to the molecule, we calculated the binding energy of the GQDs on the molecule using the following equation:

This section presents the obtained results, along with explanations and interpretation supported by previous studies. We focus on the effect of functionalizing diamond-shaped GQDs (DSGQDs) of various sizes with the therapeutic agent Mitoxantrone (
C
22
H
28
N
4
O
6
). We linked the GQDs with the MTX molecule and examined their electronic and optical properties for each case. The aim is to assess how the therapeutic molecule impacts the properties of the GQDs.

For more accuracy, we used two distinct sizes of GQDs, namely:
C
30
H
14
referred to as dibenzo-coronene and
C
48
H
18
as shown in Fig. 1. The smaller structure consists of 30 carbon atoms and 14 hydrogen atoms, whereas the larger QD comprises 48 carbon atoms and 18 hydrogen atoms. Both samples, namely
C
30
H
14
and
C
48
H
18
, are diamond-shaped, consisting of carbon atoms and dangling hydrogen bonds on the edges in order to maintain a better energetic stability of the systems, in good accordance with [67,68]. The average size of both DSGQDs is approximately 1.35 nm for the DSGQD-30 and 1.77 nm for the DSGQD-48, which aligns with our previous studies [69]. The studied QDs possess a mixture of zigzag edges and armchair corners bonded with a hydrogen saturated carbon atom. All CC bond lengths and angles values are around 1.4 Å and
12
0
°
respectively, which corresponds to the values found for graphene sheet, and aligns also with our previous work done on smaller sized C, Si and CSi QDs [69]. In addition, both structures are energetically stable, exhibiting zero negative frequencies as shown in Table 1. The results align well with theoretical studies of infrared spectra for large polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, which also report no negative frequencies [70].

Graphene quantum dots can exist in various sizes and shapes, including triangular [71], hexagonal [72], rectangular [73] and diamond [74]. Most studies on GQDs for drug delivery and other medical applications have predominantly focused on hexagonal GQDs [46,75]. The lack of prior research into diamond-shaped GQDs for drug delivery underscores the novelty and potential impact of our work. Additionally, DSGQDs offer increased structural stability compared to other geometries [32], which is advantageous for medical application. They also exhibit unique optical properties, including specific fluorescence characteristics that are advantageous for imaging applications [10].

Mitoxantrone (MTX) is a type of medicine used in chemotherapy. This organic molecule
C
22
H
28
N
4
O
6
is powerful against cancer cells, like other drugs in its group, but it is known for causing fewer heart-related side effects compared to similar drugs [76,77]. It is a synthetic antineoplastic drug related to the anthracycline antibiotic groups, composed of tricyclic planar chromophore and two basic side chains [78]. MTX is mostly used as a treatment for different types of cancer including myeloid leukemia, breast cancer, lung cancer, and prostate cancer [79,80]. The
C
22
H
28
N
4
O
6
is built with 22 carbon atoms, 28 hydrogen atoms, 4 nitrogen atoms and 6 oxygen atoms.

Given our interest in medical applications and especially cancer therapy, toxicity assessment of the selected GQDs is one of the main points that we need to focus on. Generally, the toxicity of nanomaterials like graphene is influenced by several factors including particle size, shape, surface properties, and interactions with other molecules in biological environments [81,82]. Additionally, there is multiple techniques and changes such as the bioactivity of the nanodots outer coating and mechanical stability charge that need to be considered to influence the toxicity of GQDs [83] . Overall, GQDs with diverse functional groups maintain low cytotoxicity at high concentration, in addition to a superior biocompatibility regardless of their chemical modifications, indicating good prospects for nanomedical applications [84].

Table 1 lists the DFT and TD-DFT obtained optoelectronic values for all the three structures:
C
30
H
14
,
C
48
H
18
and
C
22
H
28
N
4
O
6
. As depicted in Table 1, the smaller structure of
C
30
H
14
exhibits a significantly larger H–L energy gap value of 2.198 eV, in comparison to the larger
C
48
H
18
QD with an energy gap value of 1.219 eV, consistent with the study of the optoelectronic properties of DSGQDs [74], as well as our previous work on size engineering QDs [69]. This suggests that smaller-sized GQDs are more capable at absorbing photons with higher energies or shorter wavelengths. This phenomenon aligns with the general understanding that materials with larger band gaps are less likely to absorb lower-energy photons [85]. Therefore, the findings imply that smaller GQDs may be more efficient in harnessing sunlight, particularly in applications where absorption of higher-energy photons is advantageous such as energy storage [86].

Additionally, the electron charge density analysis presented in Fig. 2, indicates that larger QD configuration exhibit increased hybridization of frontier molecular orbitals HOMO and LUMO, as compared to the smaller QD. This behaviour likely contributes to the observed decrement in the energy gap value, which well concord with previous works [22,25,32]. The larger size of the
C
48
H
18
structure provides a greater surface area and more atomic orbitals for interaction compared to
C
30
H
14
. This leads to more extensive hybridization, bringing the HOMO and LUMO energy levels closer together. The increased orbital distribution reduces the energy gap between these levels, aligning with the findings of [87].

Lastly, for the chosen organic molecule
C
22
H
28
N
4
O
6
, the gap energy value of 2.657 eV is higher than that of both
C
30
H
14
and
C
48
H
18
QDs. This increase in energy gap can be primarily attributed to the smaller lateral size of the drug molecule compared to the larger DSGQDs. The reduced size of the drug molecule limits the extent of electron delocalization and orbital overlap, leading to a higher energy gap. In contrast, the larger DSGQDs have more extensive electronic interactions due to their greater surface area and number of atomic orbitals, which results in a smaller energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO levels. Furthermore, both LUMO and HOMO are mainly localized on the aromatic ring in the centre, indicating that the contribution to the H–L gap comes primarily from carbon atoms situated in this area.

Consistent with expectations, the smaller GQD exhibits a greater capacity for light absorption than the larger structure, with energy values of 2.345 eV for
C
30
H
14
and 1.446 eV for
C
48
H
18
configuration as shown in Table 1. This behaviour indicates that the energy gap significantly influences the optical properties, as materials with smaller H–L gaps absorb photons of lower energy, whereas those with larger band gaps need higher-energy photons for electron transitions between states. Similar behaviour and values were also observed in previous investigations into the size effects on three different sizes of DSGQDs composed with 18, 30 and 48 carbon atoms [17]. Further insights into the optical behaviour of the investigated structures are given by the absorption and emission spectra, displayed in Fig. 3. Both QDs sizes are effective at absorbing photons in both the visible and NIR regions, with the first peak being at 528.72 nm for
C
30
H
14
and 857.299 nm for the
C
48
H
18
structure. In terms of emission, it is evident that larger DSGQDs emit light at longer wavelengths compared to their smaller counterparts, consistent with their absorption behaviour. Thus, we conclude that as the size of GQDs increases, their emission shifts towards the ultraviolet end of the spectrum. These results align with previous studies, which observed that larger GQDs exhibit a redshift in the emitted light colour, moving towards the visible and longer wavelength end of the spectrum [88,89].

Additionally, the Stokes shift varies with the size of GQDs. Larger GQDs exhibit a slightly greater Stokes shift, indicating a larger difference between the absorbed and emitted light. This property makes larger GQDs especially advantageous for specific bioimaging applications.

Interestingly, the MTX molecule showed good results in term of absorption as well as emission energy values, with 2.340 eV and 1.953 eV, respectively. This molecule exhibits notable photoluminescence with an emission energy gap of 1.727 eV, suggesting that it may be capable of targeting cancer cells independently, without the need for a GQDs carrier. However, the use of GQDs is very crucial giving its importance in enhancing the fluorescence efficacy and maintaining the stability of the overall system and protecting it from degradation in various physiological conditions [90,91].

Furthermore, the stokes shift contributed to the MTX molecule was higher with respect to considered GQDs, indicating a greater loss in energy between the absorption and the emission stages. This result suggests that the effectiveness of MTX as a standalone fluorescent marker can be easily diminished. Additionally, even if the absorption and emission energies were higher and closer to the ones of the smaller QD, the oscillator strength of both absorption and emission of MTX is significantly lower compared to the QDs. This suggests that the transition between energy levels is less likely to occur. All these findings ensure the importance of using GQDs as a drug carrier.

To ensure that the GQD sizes used in this study were sufficient, we investigated the optoelectronic and optical properties of a larger GQD, specifically
C
96
H
26
with a lateral size of approximately 2.63 nm. The results showed a significant decrease in both electronic and optical gaps with values of 0.35 eV and 0.25 eV respectively, indicating that larger GQDs do not exhibit the desirable properties required for the intended application.

For generating DSGQD+MTX systems, we initially identified around 30 potential configurations within the
D
2 h
symmetry group. However, we focused on three specific configurations for this study based on particular considerations.

Considering our analysis of charge distribution within each molecule, we particularly targeted atoms with the lowest electron density in the MTX structure, specifically carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms, given that DSGQDs consist solely of carbon atoms. Notably, we observed that the least charged atoms within the DSGQDs were situated at the zigzag edges. Our selection criteria were primarily driven by the intended application, which is cancer treatment through drug delivery. Choosing atoms with minimal charge density is advantageous for facilitating the release of drug molecules from the carriers (DSGQDs) upon reaching the target site. Moreover, all generated systems were interconnected via covalent bonds, known for their moderate strength and stability, offering the necessary stability while requiring minimal energy for dissociation. Compared to Van der Waals forces, covalent bonds provide a much stronger and more direct interaction [1]. For instance, studies have shown that covalent bonding between molecules and graphene-based nanomaterials significantly enhances the stability and functionality of drug delivery systems [92]. This type of interaction ensures that the drug remains attached to the graphene carrier until it reaches the targeted site, resisting physiological conditions [93,94]. Furthermore, While Van der Waals forces may contribute to secondary interactions, they are less significant in a system where covalent bonds dominate. Those interactions are typically have much smaller interaction energies (a few kcal/mol) compared to covalent bonds, which are much stronger (100–200 kcal/mol). As such, the impact of Van der Waals forces is minor when covalent bonds are the primary focus of the study [95].

The stability of our six configurations is ensured through the examination of frequencies shown in Table 3, where all the vibrational frequencies of the conjugated systems are found to be positive, in good agreement with results found for [75]. The absence of imaginary frequencies proved that the stationary points were real minima rather than the saddle points [75].

During the functionalization process, both pristine structures of the DSGQDs were bound to the MTX molecule in three different ways, resulting in six distinct systems. First, hydrogen atoms were removed from selected atoms in each molecule to enable covalent bonding. For example, in the CC systems, a hydrogen atom was removed from a carbon atom at the edge of both sizes of DSGQDs. Next, a hydrogen atom was removed from the least charged carbon atom at the end of one wing of the Mitoxantrone. This resulted in the attachment of
C
30
H
14
and
C
48
H
18
to
C
22
H
28
N
4
O
6
via a covalent carbon–carbon bond. Next pair of systems was linked via a carbon–nitrogen bond, with carbon atoms originating from the DSGQDs. Lastly, the remaining two systems were linked via a carbon–oxygen bond, with carbon atoms also derived from the DSGQDs. This approach was used to exclude hydrogen bonding to avoid making our structures overly sensitive to factors such as pH variations, which could trigger premature drug release. This decision was based on the significant pH dependence of hydrogen bonds, as discussed in [96]. While covalent bonding was found to facilitate the adsorption and potentially enhancing the drug delivery process [96]. The same technique was used to attach and form a covalent bond between SWNTs and targeting ligands including antibodies and peptides, where a hydrogen atom was removed from both systems [97].

Fig. 4 illustrates the optimized configurations of the obtained systems using DFT. The bond lengths connecting the two configurations, namely the GQDs and the drug, were ranging between 1.37–1.52 Å after optimization. In order to provide further insights into the structural stability of the conjugated systems, the binding energy values were also determined in the gas phase, with the obtained values are presented in Table 2. Both systems exhibited similar binding energies across all three configurations, with slightly lower energy observed for the larger systems. This suggests that the
C
48
H
18
hybrid systems are more stable compared to the smaller ones. Specifically, the CO configurations appear to be the most stable in both cases. These findings align with results from previous studies on drug delivery systems [98,99].

Besides, given the importance of drug adsorption on the GQD surface, it is necessary to assess whether the selected GQD models are adequate for π - π interactions between the drug and the GQD surface. According to a study on graphene quantum dots for simultaneous fluorescence imaging and tumour-targeted drug delivery [44], graphene quantum dots, with sizes ranging from 1.5 nm to 5.5 nm, exhibit excellent biocompatibility and serve as effective carriers for anticancer drugs such as doxorubicin (DOX) through π - π stacking and hydrophobic interactions. The small size of GQDs also ensures minimal toxicity and efficient drug delivery to the target site. Another study investigated the influence of GQD size on interactions with biological molecules [100], revealing that smaller GQDs (with 23 carbon atoms) can insert into DNA molecules, whereas larger GQDs (with 52 carbon atoms) tend to adsorb onto the ends of the DNA, altering its structure. This suggests that the larger surface area of GQDs, such as
C
48
H
18
, allows for stronger π - π interactions and efficient adsorption compared to the smaller size, without drastically altering the drug’s properties or causing significant genotoxicity.

Upon DSGQDs+MTX conjugation, Table 3 presents the H–L gap energy values as well as the HOMO and LUMO energies of all the obtained structures. Interestingly, the studied systems maintained almost the same H–L gap as the pristine structures before functionalization. Specifically for systems with smaller size, we have observed that the gap values are of 2.189 eV, 2.177 eV, and 2.190 eV for the CC bond, CO bond, and CN bond systems, respectively, compared to the 2.198 eV gap energy of the
C
30
H
14
counterpart. Similarly, the larger QD exhibited H–L energy gap of 1.211 eV, 1.219 eV and 1.215 eV energy gap, for the CC bond, CO bond, and CN bond systems, respectively, compared to 1.219 eV gap energy for the pristine structure. These results indicate that functionalized systems show excellent electronic properties, even after functionalization with an organic molecule of almost the same size as the DSGQDs. This tendency of almost obtaining similar H–L energy gap values for both the pristine and the functionalized systems is also observed in a drug delivery study using nitrogen-doped GQDs as a host for a six-membered ring of the GC drug, linked using van-der-walls bonds [46]. All the investigated systems produced (with different doping pattern) with the drug had the same band gap values of the pristine QDs with a minor reduction. To get more insights into the electronic behaviour of the GQDs +MTX systems, we examined the HOMO and LUMO charge density distribution. As depicted in Fig. 5, both the HOMO and LUMO of all configurations are situated within the DSGQDs part of the system which explain the minor changes in the energy gap values. This suggests that the drug molecule may not significantly contribute to the properties of the hybridized systems since there is almost no charge transfer between the QD and this molecule MTX. Furthermore, the adjustment of HOMO and LUMO energy levels before functionalization supports this notion. As demonstrated in the previous section, the HOMO energy values of pristine DSGQDs were slightly higher than those of the MTX molecule, while the LUMO values for the DSGQDs were lower in energy compared to MTX. This disparity in energy levels between the DSGQDs and the MTX molecule could potentially influence the behaviour of the band gap following hybridization. The dominance of higher HOMOs and lower LUMOs in this context is noteworthy. Similar findings were observed in our previous study of functionalized GQDs with DOX molecule [42].

Another observation to consider is that both systems with the CO bond exhibited the largest gap energy values in both cases, while CC bond systems exhibited the lowest gap energy values. Although this observation may not be very conspicuous, it is worth mentioning. This differentiation in values based on the type of atoms involved in the covalent bond might be explained by the electronegativity existing between the two atoms in the linkage bond.

For further analysis of the physical and chemical behaviour of the hybrid systems, we calculated the global indices of reactivity for each GQDs+MTX configuration which are presented in Table 4. We first measured the chemical hardness, which is the resistance of a molecule to change its electron density. Almost all the three configurations for both cases gave similar values and results, with a slightly higher chemical hardness for CO bond, while CC bond had the lowest chemical hardness. This indicates that the configurations with higher chemical hardness need more energy for excitation, while the structures with lower chemical hardness are considered more reactive and easier to polarize and tend to easily offer electrons to an acceptor. Similar behaviour can be observed in other studies [101]. Furthermore, electrophilicity that measures the ability of a molecule to be an electron acceptor, showed also interesting results. Different results were found for both GQDs+MTX systems. The electrophilicity value of the CO bond for the larger hybrid system was slightly lower compared to the other structures (21.399 eV), which indicates a lower tendency of accepting electrons than the other structure. This also means that it is the most stable structure out of the three. However, in the smaller hybrid system, CC bond had the lowest electrophilicity (11.106 eV), followed by the CO bond with a slightly, non-noticeable increase (11.683 eV). In both cases, the CN exhibited the highest electrophilicity value, which suggests that the CN configurations are the least stable. These findings match with the previous results found for the chemical hardness and the binding energy.
In this part, we discussed the calculated absorption and emission energies, as well as the corresponding wavelengths shown in Table 3. The related spectra are illustrated in Fig. 6.

The results revealed that the absorption energy values of the configurations showed only marginal decreases. Precisely, for systems with the smaller QDs, the obtained absorption energies are 1.970 eV, 2.238 eV, and 1.915 eV for CC, CO, and CN bonds, respectively. In comparison to the 2.345 eV value of pristine configuration, these values underwent a decrease of 0.375 eV for the CC bond, 0.107 eV for the CO bond, and 0.430 eV for the CN bond. As for systems with bigger nanostructure, absorption energies were 1.237 eV, 1.438 eV and 1.419 eV for CC, CO and CN bonds, respectively. Comparing these values with the absorption energy value of 1.446 eV for the pristine structure
C
48
H
18
, a decrease ranging from 0.008 eV to 0.209 eV was observed for all three systems. Overall, functionalization appears to have a minimal impact on absorption properties. Specifically, smaller QDs primarily absorb photons within the visible spectrum
553 . 9 − 647 . 1
nm
.

Additionally, the reason behind the higher absorption value for the CO bond in both systems can be attributed to the results obtained regarding chemical hardness, electrophilicity and binding energy. As previously discussed, the CO bond was found to be the most stable structure out of the three bonds, making it less likely to be polarized and less likely to accept electrons. A stable bond is more resistant to distortion or polarization, which means it requires more energy to excite the electrons within the bond to a higher energy state, leading to a higher absorption energy value. As for the system with the CN bond, it exhibits the highest electrophilicity and the lowest chemical hardness. These results indicate that the CN bond requires less energy for electronic excitation, which might explain why the CN bonds could have lower absorption energy values compared to the CO bonds. On the other hand, the systems with the CC bond fall between the CO and CN configurations, aligning with the results found for the global indices of reactivity.

In contrast, the larger nanostructures extend their absorption into the infrared spectrum, with wavelengths ranging from 864.73 to 1001.6 nm, exhibiting a similar behaviour to that of the pristine structures.

However, varying results and values were obtained for the emission energies and wavelengths. Indeed, for the MTX conjugated to smaller QDs, distinct emission energy values were observed of 0.549 eV, 1.418 eV, and 0.923 eV corresponding to the CC, CO, and CN bonds, respectively. Notably, the CC bond displayed a substantial energy loss, exhibiting the highest Stokes shift of 1.421 eV when transitioning from absorption to emission. Conversely, larger QDs demonstrated different behaviour. They exhibited emission energies of 1.078 eV, 1.285 eV, and 0.468 eV for the CC, CO, and CN bonds, respectively. Interestingly, the CN bond experienced the most noticeable energy dissipation of 0.951 eV between absorption and emission stages. However, all systems exhibited emission peaks within the infrared spectrum, spanning a large wavelengths spectrum from 874.1 nm to 2648.9 nm, showcasing the diversity of GQDs properties. Notice that the emission wavelengths are longer than the absorption wavelengths, with absorption occurring in the visible spectrum for the smaller systems. Compared to the pristine structures, the MTX molecule primarily influences the emission behaviour, resulting in longer wavelengths and lower energies. Moreover, since MTX possesses inherent fluorescence, the MTX-GQDs combined system can provide dual functionality-therapeutic effects from MTX alongside enhanced imaging capabilities from the GQDs. This dual functionality is advantageous for tracking the drug delivery process and evaluating the distribution and accumulation of the therapeutic agent in real-time.

The UV spectra shown in Fig. 6 were also used for further analysis. Initially, we observed a shift between the absorption and emission spectra for the
C
30
H
14
+ MTX configurations, except for the
C
30
H
14
+ MTX system with CC bonds, where more pronounced differences were noted. In the photoluminescence spectra for this system, only one peak is observed, whereas the absorption and photoluminescence spectra for systems with other bonds display two peaks. This differentiation could be the reason why the
C
30
H
14
+ MTX conjugated at CC link had the lowest emission energy and the longest emission wavelengths among all three. Similarly, for the larger systems,
C
48
H
18
+ MTX with CN is the only system to exhibit a weak second peak in the emission spectra. Consequently, it had the lowest emission energy compared to the other two systems.

Overall, all the three
C
30
H
14
＋ MTX exhibited a strong first peak around 2.2, 2.3 eV which corresponds to 550–558 nm, while
C
48
H
18
＋ MTX systems exhibited peaks in the same range but with different oscillator strength. It is worth noting that the spectra describing the
C
48
H
18
+ MTX conjugated at CO, had the higher oscillator strength and also it is the system with the higher emission energy value. Moreover, the line width values corresponding to the emission spectra were analysed to gain insights into the quality of the photoluminescence obtained. Remarkably, the
C
48
H
18
+ MTX conjugate with CO bonding exhibits the smallest line width value of 0.0124 rad, indicating superior photoluminescence characteristics. This is followed by the
C
30
H
14
+ MTX conjugates with CN, CC, and CO bonding, which show line width values of approximately 0.25 eV, 0.26 eV, and 0.277 eV, respectively. Conversely, the largest line width values are observed for the
C
48
H
18
+ MTX conjugates with CN and CC bonding, which are 0.30 eV and 0.47 eV rad, respectively. These findings suggest that the
C
48
H
18
+ MTX conjugate with CO bonding demonstrates the most favourable photoluminescence properties, making it a particularly promising candidate for cancer therapy applications.

Furthermore, the unchanged photoluminescence behaviour between the pristine and hybrid systems for larger GQDs, compared to the behaviour of smaller GQD hybrid systems, suggests that the electronic characteristics of the GQDs are preserved, particularly for the CC and CO bonds, which experienced only a slight decrease in photoluminescence values, as they were found to be more stable than the CN bond. This behaviour could be attributed to the larger surface area of the
C
48
H
18
GQD, which allows for more extensive
π − π
interactions and orbital hybridization. Additionally, larger GQDs, such as
C
48
H
18
, exhibit weaker quantum confinement effects compared to smaller GQDs. This could also indicate that the electronic states in these larger GQDs are more spread out, leading to little change in their photoluminescence when Mitoxantrone is added. The charge remains mostly on the GQD, with the drug molecule having minimal impact, as shown by the HOMO–LUMO charge density. As a result, the optical properties of the larger GQD hybrid systems stay stable after functionalization.

In summary, our investigation into DSGQDs conjugated with the MTX drug unveils the remarkable optoelectronic and photoluminescence properties of these innovative nanomaterials. Employing DFT and TDDFT numerical simulation, we deeply analysed variations in GQD size and MTX-coupling positions — specifically CC, CO, and CN bonds — to understand their collective influence on the physical behaviour of DSGQDs. Our results demonstrate that all examined structures are energetically stable, as indicated by the absence of negative frequencies in the IR spectra. Besides, global reactivity indices have shown that QDs + MTX system with CO bonding are the most chemically stable. Both the size of the GQDs and the specific coupling positions significantly affect their optoelectronic properties. Functionalization notably reduces the H–L energy gap, with variations depending on the MTX coupling position and GQD size. Importantly, the intrinsic properties of the GQDs, which serve as drug carriers, remain largely unaffected by the conjugation process, showing minimal changes. The larger GQD systems maintained stable absorption and photoluminescence characteristics within the NIR range, while the smaller systems exhibited a shift from the visible to the NIR range. The
C
48
H
18
+ MTX system with CO bonding showcased the highest oscillator strength, the greatest emission energy value and the smallest line width values, emphasizing its superior photoluminescence performance, making of it the best candidate to use as cancer drug delivery system. Our findings suggest that GQDs conjugated with MTX hold significant potential for cancer drug delivery, given the minimal alterations observed between the MTX drug and the DSGQDs host. This minimal interaction indicates that the intrinsic properties of the GQDs remain largely unchanged, thereby reducing the risk of toxicity from the drug carrier. These insights, crucial for future research and development in nanomedicine, highlight the profound impact of GQD functionalization on therapeutic applications. Our work sets the stage for pioneering advancements in targeted drug delivery systems, promising a new era of precision and safety in cancer treatment.
